data.title: ANI Pharmaceuticals: Strong 2025 Finish And Bullish 2026 Guidance
data.description:
Thesis: Bullish Guidance From Management

ANI Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (ANIP) has just reported a pretty strong finish to 2025 and the stock was up about 10% as a result. Management also outlined a very ambitious growth trajectory for 2026, which tells us they’re continuing their transformation into a leading Rare Disease-focused biopharmaceutical company. Preliminary, unaudited results show that the company achieved total net revenues of about $854 to $873 million, with an adjusted non-GAAP EBITDA figure in the range of $221 to $228 million. Now, this would represent significant year-over-year growth of at least 39% and 42%, respectively, which is why the market reaction was so positive.

As you know, their rare disease segment would mainly be driven by Purified Cortrophin Gel, which we saw deliver a very impressive performance, with full-year net revenues of around $347.8 million. This figure is up 76% from 2024. Elsewhere, Iluvien and Yutiq contributed about $74.9 million combined. Looking ahead, ANI expects 2026 total net revenues to fall somewhere in the range of $1.055 to $1.115 billion, with Cortrophin Gel alone projected to hit the $540 to $575 million mark. The guidance would represent a 60% increase in total revenues, which goes to show how focused ANI is now on those high-growth therapeutic areas. I made a table below to summarize all the figures from management in the update.

To capitalize on emerging opportunities, ANI also plans a 90-person expansion, which is tipped to be dedicated to acute gouty arthritis flares and unique to Cortrophin Gel. They also have plans to advance a Phase 4 clinical trial to strengthen the evidence base for the generation of Cortrophin Gel in patients with acute gouty arthritis flares.

Elsewhere, we’re seeing the company continue to drive pretty robust execution in the generics business. They’re leveraging operational expertise and seeing a lot of upsides from a U.S.-based manufacturing footprint to sustain cash flow. They also have a pretty disciplined capital allocation strategy to support both their rare disease and pipeline initiatives. Overall, I thought ANI’s outlook is very focused on sustained revenue growth with that ambitious guidance and also looking at a few areas of strategic investment in very high-value rare disease indications, which I'll get to.

Sales On Track

In terms of Purified Cortrophin Gel, we're seeing a pretty strong acceleration in sales momentum. And I see a clear line of sight toward ANI’s FY2026 guidance. In FY2025, Cortrophin Gel generated about $347.8 million in net revenues, so the guidance would be a 76% year-over-year jump. What's more is that 4Q25 sales are at $111.4 million, which would signal an 88% year-over-year jump. It tells me the annualized exit run rate is at about $445 million. So to reach the FY2026 guidance range midpoint at $557 million, Cortrophin Gel would need to grow an additional $95 to $130 million, which would be a 21% to 29% growth figure from the Q4 exit rate. So it's a step-down from 2025 growth that leaves a lot of upsides. Very achievable with the current sales trend, but of course, continued penetration in core specialties will be needed.

Iluvien and Yutiq, on the other hand, delivered $74.9 million in FY2025 revenues, with Iluvien alone guided at the $78 to $83 million range in FY2026. So on that front, they only need about a 4% to 11% growth in that area. That would suggest stabilization rather than outsized expansion. When combined, sustained Cortrophin Gel growth remains the primary driver of the revenue outlook, while modest Iluvien/Yutiq growth is set to give incremental support to hitting total company guidance of $1.055 to $1.115 billion.

High-Value Pipeline

As I mentioned, ANI, moving forward, wants to put more effort into high-value promising areas, and one of the biggest potential catalysts would be Cortrophin Gel. Particularly for patients experiencing acute gouty arthritis flares. So going forward, the company plans to initiate a Phase 4 clinical trial designed specifically to generate additional scientific evidence/clinical data supporting the efficacy and safety of Cortrophin Gel in that specific patient population. Now, the area already has a lot of supporting studies, but this Phase 4 trial seems to be needed to strengthen the clinical foundation for its use in acute gouty arthritis. This would be an indication unique to ANI in the adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) category, and give the added benefit of supporting broader adoption.

I see Cortrophin Gel specifically for patients experiencing acute gouty arthritis flares as a big driver of growth in the near term. You see, the condition is characterized by very sudden and severe joint pain. It often comes with swelling, redness, and inflammation, which are caused by the deposition of urate crystals in the joints. As you can imagine, the flares can be intensely painful and essentially limit your overall mobility. So the upcoming trial needs evidence that Cortrophin Gel can also be effective in this area. That is, rapidly reducing inflammation and pain during the episodes. Mechanistically speaking, the gel acts as an ACTH analogue, so it stimulates the adrenal cortex to increase endogenous cortisol production, which is used to modulate immune responses by providing a potent anti-inflammatory effect.

A Word On The TAM

If we take a look at the market for acute gouty arthritis flares, it’s actually pretty significant and growing, which is probably why ANI has decided to pursue it so aggressively. There are about 9 million adults in the U.S. who live with gout, which would be about 3.9% of the adult population. And a substantial proportion of these patients experience recurrent acute flare‑ups that would need immediate treatment. Industry estimates suggest that the acute gout flare therapeutics segment alone is valued somewhere in the low‑to‑mid-billions of dollars range annually in the U.S. The broader gout therapeutics market is also projected to grow to over $4 billion globally by 2030, and a sizeable portion is thought to be driven by acute care medications. In terms of the competition, current treatment options on the market would include NSAIDs, corticosteroids, and colchicine, with branded colchicine being quite popular, with its therapies historically generating hundreds of millions in annual sales. There are also biologic and novel agents entering development alongside the current standards of care.

Risk

The one risk I see here is that the company is expected to increase dependence on Purified Cortrophin Gel as the primary growth. The gel is actually expected to account for about 60% of total company revenues by 2026. So it means any disruption in the form of reimbursement or payer pushback, supply chain or even single-source manufacturing issues could have an outsized impact on the 2026 financial performance. This risk also gets somewhat amplified when you consider the company’s reliance on single-source third-party manufacturing for Cortrophin Gel and the fact that they need to successfully execute a 90-person Rare Disease commercial expansion at the same time.

Valuation

ANI valuation looks pretty attractively priced relative to both the sector and even its own historical averages. Now, despite the small run-up from post the 2026 outlook announcement, shares are still trading at a non-GAAP forward price to earnings of 11.2x, which would be a 43% discount to the sector median and a 24% discount to its 5-year average. Elsewhere, the forward EV to EBITDA ratio sits at 9.4x and is also meaningfully below peers. We’re also still seeing this discount despite ANI giving a strong earnings growth trajectory, with consensus EPS expected to rise about 45% in 2025 and continue growing through 2028. It’s set to drive the forward price to earnings down to 8.0x by 2028.

Other than GAAP multiples, which appear quite elevated due to amortization and acquisition-related impacts, their cash-flow and sales-based metrics also hint at an attractive valuation, with price to sales at 2.0x and EV to sales at 2.4x on a forward basis. As for cash, ANI managed to end 2025 with a pretty strong liquidity position of about $285 million in cash, and given that the company is now generating substantial positive operating cash flow. With an expected $275 to $290 million in adjusted non-GAAP EBITDA this year, it isn’t cash-burning. So they effectively have a multi-year cash runway to support that Phase 4 trial going forward.

data.title: Weekly Treasury Simulation, January 9, 2026: 50,000 No-Arbitrage Heath-Jarrow-Morton Yield Scenarios
data.description:
As explained in Prof. Robert Jarrow’s book cited below, forward rates contain a risk premium above and beyond the market’s expectations for the 3-month forward rate. We document the size of that risk premium in this graph, which shows the zero-coupon yield curve implied by current Treasury prices compared with the annualized compounded yield on 3-month Treasury bills that market participants would expect based on the daily movement of government bond yields in 14 countries since 1962. The risk premium, the reward for a long-term investment, is large and widens over most of the full 30-year maturity range. The graph also shows a decline in expected yields at a steady pace for the full 30 years. We explain the details below.

For more on this topic, see the analysis of government bond yields in 14 countries through November 30, 2025, given in the appendix.

Inverted Yields, Negative Rates, and U.S. Treasury Probabilities 10 Years Forward

The negative 2-year/10-year Treasury spread ended on August 26, 2024, after 537 trading days. The spread is currently at positive 0.64%. The table below shows that the August 26, 2024, streak of inverted yield curves is the longest in the U.S. Treasury market since the 2-year Treasury yield was first reported on June 1, 1976. The second longest streak is 423 trading days, starting on August 18, 1978.

In this week’s forecast, the focus is on three elements of interest rate behavior: the future probability of the recession-predicting inverted yield curve, the probability of negative rates, and the probability distribution of U.S. Treasury yields over the next decade.

We start from the closing U.S. Treasury yield curve published daily by the U.S. Department of the Treasury. Using a maximum smoothness forward rate approach, Friday’s implied forward rate curve shows the shortest maturity 1-month forward rates at 3.70%, versus 3.71% last week. There is a decline followed by a gradual rise until rates peak at 5.97%, compared to 6.03% the previous week. Forward rates stabilize at 4.68% at the 30-year horizon, versus 4.72% last week.

Using the methodology outlined in the appendix, we simulate 50,000 future paths for the U.S. Treasury yield curve out to thirty years. The next three sections summarize our conclusions from that simulation.

Inverted Treasury Yields: Positive Spread Now, 24.7% Probability by December 23, 2039

Many economists have concluded that a downward-sloping U.S. Treasury yield curve is an important indicator of future recessions. A recent example is this paper by Alex Domash and Lawrence H. Summers. We measure the probability that the 10-year par coupon Treasury yield is lower than the 2-year par coupon Treasury for every scenario in each of the first 40 quarterly periods in the simulation.[1] The next graph shows that the probability of an inverted yield now peaks at 24.7%, compared to 25.2% one week before, in the 91-day period ending December 23, 2039.

Negative Treasury Bill Yields: 11.2% Probability by December 13, 2047

The next graph describes the probability of negative 3-month Treasury bill rates for all but the first 3 months of the next three decades. The probability of negative rates starts near zero and then peaks at 11.2% in the 91-day period ending December 13, 2047.

Calculating the Default Risk from Interest Rate Maturity Mismatches

In light of the interest-rate-risk-driven failure of Silicon Valley Bank on March 10, 2023, we have added a table that applies equally well to banks, institutional investors, and individual investor mismatches from buying long-term Treasury bonds with borrowed short-term funds. We assume that the sole asset is a 10-year Treasury bond purchased at time zero at par value of $100. We analyze default risk for four different initial market value of equity to market value of asset ratios: 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%. For the banking example, we assume that the only class of liabilities is deposits that can be withdrawn at par at any time. In the institutional and retail investor case, we assume that the liability is essentially a borrowing on margin/repurchase agreement with the possibility of margin calls. For all investors, the amount of liabilities (95, 90, 85 or 80) represents a “strike price” on a put option held by the liability holders. Failure occurs via a margin call, bank run, or regulatory takeover (in the banking case) when the value of assets falls below the value of liabilities.

The graph below shows the cumulative 10-year probabilities of failure for each of the 4 possible capital ratios when the asset’s maturity is 10 years. For the 5 percent case, that default probability is 49.31%, compared to 49.80% from last week.

This default probability analysis is updated weekly based on the U.S. Treasury yield simulation described in the next section. The calculation process is the same for any portfolio of assets with credit risk included.

U.S. Treasury Probabilities 10 Years Forward

In this section, the focus turns to the decade ahead. This week’s simulation shows that the most likely range for the 3-month U.S. Treasury bill yield in ten years is from 1% to 2%, unchanged from last week. There is a 22.22% probability that the 3-month yield falls in this range, compared to 21.96% one week before. For the 10-year Treasury yield, the most likely range is from 2% to 3%, unchanged from last week. The probability of being in this range is 21.13%, compared to 20.80% one week prior.

In a recent post on SeekingAlpha, we pointed out that a forecast of “heads” or “tails” in a coin flip leaves out critical information. What a sophisticated bettor needs to know is that, on average for a fair coin, the probability of heads is 50%. A forecast that the next coin flip will be “heads” is literally worth nothing to investors because the outcome is purely random.

The same is true for interest rates.

In this section we present the detailed probability distribution for both the 3-month Treasury bill rate and the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield 10 years forward using semi-annual time steps.[2] We present the probability of where rates will be at each time step in one percent “rate buckets.” The forecast for 3-month Treasury yields is shown in this graph:

3-Month U.S. Treasury Yield Data Are Attached.

SAS3monthUST20260109.xlsx

The probability that the 3-month Treasury bill yield will be between 1% and 2% in 2 years is shown in column 4: 26.28%. The probability that the 3-month Treasury bill yield will be negative (as it has been often in Europe and Japan) in 2 years is 1.92% plus 0.06% plus 0.00% = 1.98% (difference due to rounding). Cells shaded in blue represent positive probabilities of occurring, but the probability has been rounded to the nearest 0.01%. The shading scheme is defined as follows:

  • Dark blue: the probability is greater than 0% but less than 1%
  • Light blue: the probability is greater than or equal to 1% and less than 5%
  • Light yellow: the probability is greater than or equal to 5% and 10%
  • Medium yellow: the probability is greater than or equal to 10% and less than 20%
  • Orange: the probability is greater than or equal to 20% and less than 25%
  • Red: the probability is greater than 25%

The chart below shows the same probabilities for the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield derived as part of the same simulation.

10-Year US Treasury Yield Data Are Attached.

SAS10yearUST20260109.xlsx

Modeling Correlated Foreign Exchange Rates and Government Yields

The SAS HJM++© methodology is based on five decades of research by Prof. Robert Jarrow. Using the no-arbitrage conditions of Heath, Jarrow, and Morton [1992] and foreign exchange rate no-arbitrage conditions from Amin and Jarrow [1992], foreign exchange rates and government yields in major markets are simulated jointly. The simulation is based on daily movements in government yields and foreign exchange rates since January 2, 1962. The resulting distribution of the yen-U.S. dollar exchange rate one year forward is shown in this graph:

Similarly, the forward-looking correlation between 10-year zero-coupon Bund and U.S. Treasury yields is shown below:

For more information on HJM++ scenario generation and no-arbitrage Monte Carlo simulation, please contact the author.

Appendix: Treasury Simulation Methodology

The Treasury yield probabilities are derived using the same methodology that SAS Institute Inc. recommends to its KRIS® and SAS ALM/Kamakura Risk Manager® clients. A moderately technical explanation is given later in the appendix, but we summarize it in plain English first.

Step 1: We take the closing U.S. Treasury yield curve as our starting point.

Step 2: We use the number of points on the yield curve that best explains historical yield curve shifts. Using daily government bond yield data from 14 countries from 1962 through November 30, 2025, we conclude that 12 “factors” drive almost all movements of government bond yields. The countries on which the analysis is based are Australia, Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, and New Zealand. Russia, Singapore, Spain, Sweden, Thailand, the United Kingdom, and the United States of America. No data from Russia is included after January 2022. The graph below shows which points on the zero-coupon yield curve are incorporated as risk factors and the order in which they are added to the model:

Step 3: We measure the volatility of changes in those factors and how volatility has changed over the same period.

Step 4: Using those measured volatilities, we generate 50,000 random shocks at each time step and derive the resulting yield curve.

Step 5: We “validate” the model to make sure that the simulation EXACTLY prices the starting Treasury curve and that it fits history as well as possible. The methodology for doing this is described below.

Step 6: We take all 50,000 simulated yield curves and calculate the probabilities that yields fall in each of the 1% “buckets” displayed in the graph.

Do Treasury Yields Accurately Reflect Expected Future Inflation?

We showed in a recent post on SeekingAlpha that, on average, investors have almost always done better by buying long-term bonds than by rolling over short-term Treasury bills. That means that market participants have generally (but not always) been accurate in forecasting future inflation and adding a risk premium to that forecast.

The distribution above helps investors estimate the probability of success from going long.

Finally, as mentioned weekly in the Corporate Bond Investor Friday overview, the future expenses (both the amount and the timing) that all investors are trying to cover with their investments are an important part of investment strategy. The author follows his own advice: cover the short-term cash needs first and then step out to cover more distant cash needs as savings and investment returns accumulate.

Technical Details

Daily government bond yields from the 14 countries listed above form the base historical data for fitting the number of yield curve factors and their volatility. The U.S. historical data is provided by the U.S. Department of the Treasury. The use of the international bond data increases the number of observations to more than 109,000 and provides a more complete range of experience with both high rates and negative rates than a U.S. data set alone provides.

The modeling process was published in a very important paper by David Heath, Robert Jarrow, and Andrew Morton in 1992:

For technically inclined readers, we recommend Prof. Jarrow’s book Modeling Fixed Income Securities and Interest Rate Options for those who want to know exactly how the “HJM” model construction works.

The number of factors, 12 for the 14-country model, has been stable since June 30, 2017.

Footnotes:

[1] After the first 20 years in the simulation, the 10-year Treasury cannot be derived from the initial 30 years of Treasury yields.

[2] The full simulation uses 91-day time steps for 30 years forward. This note summarizes just the first 10 years of the full simulation.

data.title: Postal Realty Trust: Growth, High Dividends, And Low Valuation
data.description:
I'm initiating coverage of Postal Realty Trust (PSTL), which I see as a steady growth REIT with a strong dividend yield. The REIT's low valuation leaves more room to the upside for the stock.

Postal Realty Trust Business Background

Postal Realty Trust operates as a $545 million market cap New York-based realty investment trust [REIT]. The REIT is focused on acquiring/managing properties that are mostly leased to the United States Postal Service [USPS]. This includes postal industrial facilities and post offices. As of September 30, 2025, PSTL owned a portfolio of 1,853 properties. However, PSTL just acquired 65 additional properties leased to USPS on January 8, 2026. The 65 properties were purchased for $29.1 million and they comprise 142k of net leasable interior square feet. This brings PSTL's total portfolio of properties up to 1,917 for about $7.1 million of leasable interior square feet.

PSTL obtains its revenue from rent and tenant reimbursements. These are through leases with the USPS and from fees from managing the properties.

As a REIT, Postal Realty Trust doesn't pay Federal income tax on the net taxable income that is paid to shareholders. PSTL is required to pay a minimum of 90% of taxable income to shareholders under the REIT code. PSTL currently has an annual dividend yield of about 6%.

Postal Realty Trust's Growth Catalysts

Postal Realty has multiple factors that act as growth catalysts. PSTL has a three main strategies that it employs to drive growth and to reward shareholders. The first strategy is to drive organic growth within the REIT's portfolio. This is accomplished by "programmatic leasing with the Postal Service." This involves the process of negotiating new leases for the existing owned properties and for future acquisitions.

The 2nd strategy involves sourcing and acquiring postal properties that can be immediately accretive to the bottom line. PSTL also targets properties that can "become significantly more accretive over time." Postal Realty's 3rd strategy involves "increasing access to capital to fund the accretive growth."

These strategies led PSTL to achieve a 23% yoy revenue increase to $24.3 million. This exceeded Postal Realty's expectations. The results were attributed to "faster lease executions with the USPS" and from fees/other income exceeding expectations.

PSTL also achieved a 10% yoy increase in AFFO to $0.33 per share in Q3 2025. Postal Realty increased its AFFO (adjusted funds from operations) per share guidance for 2025 by $0.06 to a range of $1.30 to $1.32. This would result in 13% annual AFFO growth. PSTL believes that it has good visibility for revenue growth for 2026 and will provide that guidance in the Q4 2025 earnings call scheduled for February 27, 2026.

Postal Realty is guiding total 2025 acquisitions to meet or exceed $110 million. PSTL makes acquisitions when the cost of capital makes sense. This strategy is what enables PSTL's acquisitions to be immediately accretive. Once an acquisition is made, PSTL strives to "increase value to the Postal Service as an efficient point of contact." At the same time, PSTL drives organic growth for shareholders by discovering good lease opportunities with higher annual rent potential.

PSTL also decreased operating expenses by 2.7% from Q3 2024 to Q3 2025. This led to the operating margin to increase from 25.4% to 37.9% over the same time. This was a result of "the timing and scope of repairs and maintenance projects."

Strong Balance Sheet/Cash Flow

One of the reasons why the REIT is successful is because of PSTL's strong balance sheet. At the end of Q3 2025, "net debt to annualized adjusted EBITDA was 5.2x." The weighted average debt maturity was 3.5 years while the fixed rate debt comprised 93% of PSTL's borrowings.

PSTL has total assets of $735 million and total liabilities of $387 million for total equity of $348.5 million. Postal Realty's total buildings have a worth of $581 million. PSTL also has land worth about $158 million. With an asset to liability ratio of about 1.9x, PSTL is in great shape to handle its debt obligations.

Adding to the balance sheet strength is PSTL's strong cash flow. PSTL had $42.4 million in operating cash flow over the trailing 12 months. Postal Realty increased its operating cash flow every year since at least 2017. The operating cash flow increased 36% yoy to $10.9 million in Q3 2025. This strong cash flow growth puts PSTL in great shape to continue its real estate acquisitions.

Valuation

I'm using the price/AFFO ratio since it is a standard valuation metric for REITs. Postal Realty Trust is trading with a forward price/AFFO of 12.4. This is below the sector median of 15.4. So, the REIT is trading with a slight undervaluation.

PSTL is expected to grow revenue at 16.5% to $106 million and FFO per share at 5.3% to $1.36 in 2026 according to analysts' consensus estimates. PSTL has been growing revenue at double-digit rates for multiple years. This is expected to continue over the next few years. Postal Realty's strategy of acquiring new properties for rental income should continue to drive this strong growth.

The chart above shows how PSTL's price has been lagging its FFO over the past few years. This tells me that the price has some catching up to do. I expect the REIT's price to rise steadily in 2026 as PSTL continues its growth from this low valuation level.

Technical Perspective

Postal Realty's weekly chart shows a strong uptrend. The REIT is bullish as the RSI remains above the 50 level. The MACD confirms the bullish trend as the blue line is above the red signal line while the histogram bars turned green.

While the stock doesn't go up in a straight line with pullbacks along the way, I expect the overall upward trend to continue in 2026 and beyond. PSTL has a solid strategy for sustainable growth over the long-term. This should drive strong revenue and cash flow growth, which should drive the stock higher.

The Q4 earnings report scheduled for February 27, 2026 could be the next strong catalyst for Postal Realty. News of property acquisitions could be another catalyst.

Risks for Postal Realty Trust

Postal Realty Trust highly depends on the demand for leased postal properties. PSTL derives all of its revenue from properties that are leased to the USPS. Decreases in the demand for these properties would have a significant negative impact on PSTL's revenue and AFFO. Demand for postal properties could change due to changes in government spending, increased competition from other delivery companies, etc.

The USPS has a ten-year plan to address the shift from letter delivery to package delivery. Issues to be addressed include: "the declining financial situation, processing underperformance, retail/delivery operations, and transportation." It is possible that this plan could negatively impact PSTL if USPS stopped renewing some of its leases that are owned by the REIT.

It is possible that PSTL fails to acquire new properties at the pace that it intends to over the long-term. This could lead to less than expected growth and negatively impact revenue, AFFO, and the stock price. The amount of properties that are leased to the USPS is limited. So, PSTL could run into challenges with its acquisition plans.

Postal Realty Trust's Long-Term Outlook

Postal Realty has a positive long-term growth outlook. The REIT has a sound strategy to acquire and lease USPS properties. So far, there is no other publicly traded REIT that does this. PSTL has a great multiple-year track record of growing revenue at an above-average double-digit annual pace. This is expected to continue in 2026 and beyond.

I expect PSTL's strong revenue growth, margin expansion, and AFFO growth to continue to drive the stock higher at an above-average pace. The low valuation level should help support above-average gains. The attractive dividend yield of 6% is also likely to be supported by this growth. PSTL increased its dividends for six straight years since its IPO in 2019. So, I expect this track record to continue.

data.title: Under Armour: A Bad Bet As Market Share Issues Deepen
data.description:
So far, in early 2026, markets are fighting off all bearish triggers. Despite overheated valuations, uncertain macroeconomic footing, and the latest tussle between the White House and Fed Chair Powell, the S&P 500 remains trading near all-time highs.

But this isn’t a time to be complacent. In my view, visual stock picking is more important than ever, and we have to be especially careful to avoid stocks that are losing the most market share in their industries. Under Armour, Inc. (UA) is particularly at risk. The stock is down nearly 30% over the past year, but in December, experienced a ~20% burst from lows that I consider to be a phantom rally with weak fundamental justification.

I’m initiating Under Armour at a "Sell" rating.

Weak Sales Data That Reflects Waning Market Share

As most investors are aware, Under Armour was founded 30 years ago in 1996. Its defining product, and what customers still associate the brand with today, is a sweat-wicking, mesh material that has since become more or less the standard for athletic shirts and performance apparel.

But unfortunately, Under Armour has fallen from being one of the most premium brands in sportswear to an afterthought. The top item on the bear case for Under Armour is persistently weak sales performance. The company’s fiscal Q2 (September quarter) earnings results are shown below:

Under Armour’s revenue in Q2 fell 5% y/y to $1.33 billion, which worsened slightly from -4% growth in Q1.

We note that while Under Armour is showcasing growth and a revival in Europe, its largest market in the U.S. has declined 8% y/y. Meanwhile, Asia, which was one of the company’s most meaningful growth drivers, fell 14% y/y.

There’s an element of wholesale retraction here as well. Wholesale revenue fell 6% y/y, underperforming direct-to-consumer revenue of -2%. This is an indication that retailers are wary of taking on slow-moving inventory in a tougher macro climate.

Where we are particularly concerned, however, is Under Armour’s loss of market share, which can be implied from recent trends. We note that for all the fanfare around Nike’s recent fall from grace, Nike is actually faring a lot better than Under Armour. The latest results from Nike (through the November quarter) show 1% overall growth, with 4% growth in apparel. Apparel is the most direct comparison for Under Armour, as its presence in footwear is smaller relative to its clothing line.

It’s not just Nike, either. Again, Lululemon (LULU) has been cited to be losing share, particularly among the core U.S. women’s demographic, and yet the Canadian athletic giant is still showing better results than Under Armour. Lululemon grew 7% in the most recent quarter, especially driven by 47% currency-neutral growth in mainland China, its fastest-growing region. Meanwhile, Under Armour counts Asia as its fastest-declining region.

In more recent news, Lululemon also cited strong holiday sales for the fourth quarter and lifted its operating margin guidance. All of these are clear indicators that Under Armour is losing share.

Adding insult to injury, Under Armour’s gross margins are falling. In Q3, gross margin of 47.3% fell 250bps y/y. Now, of course, there’s an element of tariff impact in this comparison. Across the board, Nike and Lululemon are also seeing margins peel away.

But the bigger concern to me is that falling margins across the whole space indicate that the sector is also having to lean more deeply into promotions to keep customers satisfied. Among its competitors, Under Armour is the weakest positioned to absorb a profit hit.

Not Doing Enough On Cost To Adjust To Its Weaker Reality

To me, the nail in the coffin for Under Armour is that the company isn’t reacting quickly enough or deeply enough to adjust to its slower sales reality.

Fads come and go in the apparel space. Once momentum is lost, it’s very difficult to reclaim. We only have to look at the deep graveyard of fallen apparel and retail stocks to confirm that reality. But it’s much harder to peel back a corporate bureaucracy, once it’s been built.

In Q3, Under Armour’s selling, general, and administrative expenses grew at 12% y/y, and even excluding $4 million of one-time payments bucketed here for restructuring, the company’s adjusted SG&A would still have grown 9% y/y. Further adjusting for a $27 million positive insurance recovery last year, the company notes SG&A would have been roughly flat. Still, it’s not good enough.

In 2025, Under Armour announced a broad restructuring plan. Updating investors on the latest on this plan, the company notes it achieved $35 million of run rate cost savings in 2025, and is on track to achieve $45 million more in 2026. Per CFO David Bergman’s remarks on the most recent earnings call:

Since the plan's inception, we have incurred $147 million in charges and transformation expenses, of which $82 million are cash related and $65 million are noncash. We expect total charges and expenses for the plan to be up to $160 million, which will be recognized by the end of fiscal 2026. The actions executed under our plan have already delivered approximately $35 million in savings in fiscal 2025 and are on track to generate an additional $45 million in fiscal '26.

We think spending $160 million in one-time charges to achieve $80 million of annual savings, with a payback period of more than two years considering the linearity of savings achievement is back-end loaded, is not entirely effective. Also, when we consider Under Armour’s $5 billion in annualized revenue, $80 million of annualized savings translates to less than a 2% improvement in operating margins.

In my view, Under Armour should be cutting costs at a much deeper pace to reflect its 5% y/y revenue decline and market share losses. Perhaps the fact that the company isn’t cutting deeper is a reflection that it’s unable to, and its structural profitability at its shrunken revenue scale is much weaker.

Valuation And Key Takeaways

Despite a company in crisis, Under Armour still sits at what I consider to be an unrealistic valuation multiple.

As shown above, Under Armour's ~15x forward EBITDA multiple is cheaper only than Nike (NKE), which has retained its legacy premium. Still, Lululemon is trading substantially cheaper despite recent growth trends, and beyond sports and performance apparel, there are other names like Gap (GAP) and Urban Outfitters (URBN) trading even cheaper.

Steer clear here and invest elsewhere. With Under Armour’s share losses and weak attempts at cost mitigation, its recent uptrend isn’t likely to be sustained very long.

data.title: SCHD: Market Rotation Beneficiary, Dividend And Capital Appreciation Story Still Compelling
data.description:
I previously covered Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) in October 2025, discussing how the ETF had missed the ongoing AI mania, albeit with it triggering the expanded dividend yield of 3.76% compared to the 5Y mean of 3.27%.

Combined with the strategic portfolio reshuffling, the ETF had been able to deliver a quality and sustainable dividend story, as observed in the LTM payout growth by +6.1% sequentially, with it resulting in my reiterated Buy rating for dividend-oriented investors seeking to add to their well-diversified income portfolio.

Since then, SCHD has outperformed with a total return of +8.4% compared to the wider market at +5.1%, thanks to the recent AI-related meltdown arising from valuations/debt risks triggering a notable market rotation to deep-value/dividend-oriented plays.

The capital appreciation has directly contributed to SCHD's notably lower forward dividend yields from the recent highs of 4.27% to 3.67% by the time of writing, albeit still more than decent against the 5Y mean of 3.36% and the 10Y mean of 3.14%.

Even so, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my buy rating for SCHD here, thanks to its diversified exposure across different sectors, the richer yields compared to some passively managed dividend oriented ETFs, and the prospects of further upside potential.

Focus On Value-Oriented Holdings

SCHD's focus as a dividend oriented ETF naturally relies on its holdings that offer rich/secure payouts, with one such stock being Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (BMY).

It is unsurprising that BMY has emerged as SCHD's top holding, thanks to its inherently discounted valuations at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 8.55x and the consequently richer forward dividend yields of 4.51%, compared to the sector median of 19.70x and 1.31%, respectively.

The discount is attributed to BMY being a legacy pharmaceutical company, with the management having to renew its growth prospects by embarking on numerous M&A activities to mitigate the risks from its patent cliff headwinds, with over 40% of its revenue at risks.

For now, I reckon that BMY has been rather successful indeed, since its Growth Portfolio already comprises 56.5% of its overall top lines in FQ3'25 (+7.8 points YoY), with the accelerating Growth Portfolio at +17% YoY well balancing the Legacy Portfolio's deterioration at -13% YoY despite the tougher comparison from a year ago at +20% YoY and +1% YoY, respectively.

While I expect a net neutral impact on its overall revenue performance, instead of net positive, I am of the opinion that these efforts already demonstrate BMY's ability to remain resilient in the face of patent cliff while continuing to pay out rich and secure dividends, thanks to the robust cash flows and the healthier balance sheet.

At the same time, Verizon (VZ) reclaiming its position as one of the top ten holdings in SCHD is unsurprising as well (compared to last article in October 2025), as the highest yielding dividend stock with forward yields of 6.82% against other holdings' at low single digits, with the telecom stock notably carrying much of the ETF's income investment thesis.

If anything, if not for the recent market rotation to value-oriented stocks, readers must note that BMY and VZ previously boasted even richer forward dividend yields of 5.82% and 7.09%, well beyond the wider market's yield at 1.05%.

Despite being a dividend oriented ETF, readers may also look forward to SCHD's compelling capital appreciation prospect ahead, barring portfolio reshuffling, since both BMY (as discussed above) and VZ are embarking on notable portfolio renewals to higher growth/margins offerings.

A similar trend has also been observed in VZ, with the telecom company already engaging on an aggressive headcount reduction and operational restructuring to franchised retail operations under the new CEO.

With the new CEO already touting how “cost reductions will be a way of life for us here” and the strategy is expected to fund their “significant investment” towards reclaiming their market leadership, I am of the opinion that VZ is likely to report improved productivity and profitability from FY2026 onwards.

Combined with VZ's inherent undervaluation at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 8.63x against the sector median of 16.14x, along with the rich cash flows well covering their capex intensive operations, the ongoing deleveraging target, and naturally, their dividend obligations, I am of the opinion that VZ's position as one of SCHD's top ten holdings is deserved indeed.

Risks From Energy Sector's Ongoing Volatilities

On the other hand, while I was previously optimistic about SCHD's increased exposure to the energy sector, currently at 20.44% of its holdings, things might not be the same now.

My caution is attributed to the ongoing OPEC+ raised outputs and the growing US crude oil output, amongst others, with it already contributing to the International Energy Agency's [IEA] estimated global oil supply growth to 108.6 m barrels per day in 2026 and the US Energy Information Administration's [EIA] projection of "global oil inventory builds will exceed 2 million barrels per day (b/d) in 2026."

As a result of the potential supply glut, it is unsurprising that the US EIA has projected that Brent oil prices may further drop to "an average of $55/b in the first quarter of 2026 (1Q26) and will stay near that price for the rest of the year," down notably from current spot prices of $63.34 per barrel as of January 09, 2026.

This is worsened by the recent conflict in Venezuela, a country with "the world's largest oil reserves, bigger even than those of OPEC's leader Saudi Arabia," as the US President "are planning a sweeping initiative to dominate the Venezuelan oil industry for years to come" with "his efforts could help lower oil prices to his favored level of $50 a barrel, according to people familiar with the matter."

The drastically lower prices of $50 per barrel is likely to trigger narrower spreads and top-line/cash flow headwinds for many (if not all) oil and gas players, notably ConocoPhillips (COP) as an upstream player at 4.17% of SCHD's holdings and Chevron (CVX) to a smaller extent at 4.07%, attributed to the latter's diversified upstream/downstream offerings.

For reference, COP has previously highlighted their breakeven price point of approximately $55 per barrel, based on "our breakeven this year, so just on CapEx is in the mid-40s, you'd add another $10 to that for the dividend," compared to CVX's slightly lower break even price point at <$50 per barrel.

As a result of their potentially lower cash flows and consequently, impacted dividend payouts upon that speculative event, I urge readers to observe the ongoing developments for a little longer, with the expanded exposure to the energy sector seemingly being a headwind to SCHD's dividend investment thesis now.

SCHD Remains A Great Buy

Thanks to the outsized upward momentum by +7.9% since the November 2025 bottom and +17.3% since the April 2025 bottom, it is unsurprising then, that much of SCHD's portfolio is seemingly overbought now, with only three of its sectors currently undervalued, namely, the Energy, Technology, Communications, or the equivalent of 34.1% of the ETF's holdings.

This is based on Morningstar's Price/Fair Value by Sector metrics, with the market rotation seemingly overly done while triggering a minimal margin of safety.

Otherwise, assuming further volatility in the energy sector, readers may look forward to a portfolio re-balancing soon, with it likely to mitigate the risks from the fluctuating spot prices while securing the safety of the ETF's future dividend payouts, similar to the prior removal of Broadcom (AVGO) during the prior AI boom.

A similar trend has been observed in SCHD seemingly on its path to retest the prior November 2024 highs of $29s along with the overbought RSIs, with it presenting risks for those looking to add here, since it remains to be seen when a reversal in market sentiments may occur.

As a dividend ETF, it goes without saying that the moderated yields trigger headwinds to its investment thesis, albeit with a similar trend also observed in some of its passively managed ETF peers, including:

  • iShares Select Dividend ETF (DVY) at forward dividend yields of 3.53% against the recent highs of 4.09%,
  • SPDR S&P Dividend ETF (SDY) at 2.53%/2.87%, and
  • Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM) at 2.38%/3.21%, respectively,

with it apparent that the recent market rotation has occurred across the board, with a similar trend also observed in numerous actively managed, dividend-oriented ETFs (including those using options/covered calls) to generate higher dividend yields, including:

  • Neos S&P 500(R) High Income ETF (SPYI) at 11.55%/14.37%,
  • Global X S&P 500® Covered Call ETF (XYLD) at 10.42%/13.94%, and
  • JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) at 8.10%/8.65%, respectively.

Part of the market rotation may also be attributed to Fed's numerous rate cuts, with it already triggering the moderation in the US Treasury Yields to between 3.50% and 4.81% by the time of writing, down from the peak of 4.95% and 5.51% observed in October 2023, respectively, as more dividend oriented investors piled into dividend stocks/ETFs, SCHD included.

Does this mean that I am downgrading the SCHD stock to a Hold here?

Not quite.

I am of the opinion that SCHD remains a compelling, dividend oriented ETF for investors seeking secure, quarterly payouts in their well-diversified income portfolios.

This is especially since SCHD's 3-for-1 share split in October 2024 already triggers a more compelling entry point for those looking to initiate small positions.

Combined with the rich yields as a passively managed dividend ETF, the ETF trading close to its NAV of $28.54, and the robust dividend growth at a 5Y CAGR of +9.1% (compared to the SPY at +5.05%), I am reiterating my Buy rating for SCHD.

Interested investors may observe the ETF price movement for a little longer before adding, preferably upon a slight retracement for an expanded forward dividend yield of nearing ~4%.

data.title: RQI: Positioned For Success As Data Center Growth Expands
data.description:
Overview

Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns through its diverse portfolio of real estate securities. When I previously covered RQI, I issued a buy rating due to the efficient fund strategy and resilience despite challenging market conditions. Now that we've crossed into the new year, I wanted to revisit the fund's value proposition and its ability to support its distributions going forward. Despite a challenging interest rate environment, the fund continues to demonstrate resilience and outperforms more traditional sector-tracking ETFs.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that RQI's share price has declined by about 4.55%. Despite the weakness, I think this is understandable considering the disappointing growth of the real estate sector. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 4.46% over the same time frame. RQI now provides investors with a starting dividend yield of about 9.2% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. RQI has also maintained a long track record of consistent distributions, which is great for investors seeking stable income.

When compared to peers, I believe that RQI is best suited to participate in the direct equity growth of the real estate sector. There are several different Cohen & Steers real estate funds to choose from, but RQI's greater focus on common equities aligns the fund for growth in the future. Additionally, RQI maintained an efficient exposure to data center REITs, which may see rapid growth in the future due to the growing demand of AI data centers. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that RQI implements to generate its earnings for shareholders.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, RQI has total managed assets of $2.5B that is spread across ~193 different positions. The fund's primary goal is to seek high current income through its portfolio of real estate securities. To achieve this, the fund implements a blended approach between common equities and preferred/fixed income investments. This blend allows RQI to directly participate in the growth of the real estate sector, while also generating income during market cycles that may be less favorable. The structure by asset type is as follows:

  • Common Equities: 82%.
  • Preferred/Fixed Income: 18%.

What I like about RQI is that the fund provides instant diversification among the many sub-sectors of real estate. For instance, we can see that RQI is most heavily allocated to REITs within the Health Care sector at 15% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to Telecommunications and Data Center REITs, which accounts for 13% and 10% of assets respectively. We can also see that RQI has an allocation to Corporate Bonds and something classified as 'Other', which may consist of the various income securities.

I really like the fact that RQI has exposure to data centers because this has the potential to be a growth catalyst over the long term. With the rising demand of different AI technologies, data centers are also in growing demand. There needs to be more data centers to warehouse the growing infrastructure and power needs, so RQI is directly aligned to participate in this growth. For instance, Grand View Research estimates that the size of the AI data center market can increase at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 28.3% through 2030.

When looking at the holdings within RQI, we can see that the fund has exposure to many notable REITs within the sector. However, RQI is a bit concentrated on the top ten holdings, so this may cause a bit more volatility if the sector faces some headwinds. The top ten positions make up 47.6% of total assets. The largest position is currently Welltower (WELL) with a weight of 10.3%. This is followed by many other notable REITs such as American Tower (AMT), Digital Realty Trust (DLR), Crown Castle (CCI), Equinix (EQIX), and Iron Mountain (IRM), just to name a few.

Like most real estate funds, RQI also utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify its earnings. The use of leverage currently amounts to 29% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$725M. I consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage in this higher interest rate environment, since the inclusion of debt can increase the fund's overall risk profile. On the bright side, 81% of this leverage is structured on a fixed rate basis, with a weighted average rate on fixed financing of 2.5%. The remaining 19% of leverage is structured on a variable rate of 4.9%, which is a lot more costly.

The inclusion of leverage means that RQI may introduce some sensitivity to interest rates. Despite the Fed initiating three different 25 basis point cuts to interest rates, the federal funds rate still remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. As we can see below, RQI was able to show a greater level of capital appreciation when interest rates were sitting near zero levels between 2020 and 2021. A lower cost of debt translates to a healthier operating spread for RQI between its cost of debt and the yield collected from its securities. So assuming that interest rates continue to trend downward over the next twelve months, RQI may be aligned to see much better performance going forward.

Performance Insights & Comparison

However, RQI's emphasis on income generation has some tradeoffs that must be considered. Since RQI pays out such large distributions, the fund may need to actively sell off the upside growth of some of its positions, which means that capital appreciation may be limited. Additionally, the fund initiates an option writing strategy against a small portion of its assets in order to amplify the income received. The option premiums received from these written options are used to help fund the higher payouts.

For instance, the latest Q3 holding update, which is the latest available insight into its holdings, reveals how the option positions work. However, I just wanted to provide this as additional insight to help lay the framework for how the option strategy works conceptually. All of these option contracts are now expired, but we can use it as a reference to see how the notional amount of $53.7M is a very small portion of RQI's total assets of $2.5B.

When measuring the performance against a more traditional REIT ETF, such as the State Street Real Estate Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLRE), we can see how RQI underperforms from a price perspective. RQI's share price has only increased by 1% over the last decade, while XLRE's share price has increased by 34.5% over the same time frame. However, RQI outperforms when we look at its total return of 124.4% over the last ten years, which includes all dividends that were paid out to shareholders. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from RQI's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions over time.

However, there are a few different peers from Cohen & Steers that investors can choose from. While their underlying strategies may differ in terms of sector allocation, leverage, and position weights, the underlying premise of providing income generation from a pool of real estate securities remains similar. The two funds that can serve as suitable alternatives are the following peers:

  • Cohen & Steers REIT & Preferred Income Fund (RNP): ~8% dividend yield.
  • Cohen & Steers Real Estate Opportunities and Income Fund (RLTY): ~8.9% dividend yield.

As we can see, RQI still offers the highest dividend yield of the peer group, and it is also the largest in terms of total assets under management. The standout feature of both of these peers is that they both have a lower allocation to common equities compared to RQI. Approximately 52% of RNP's assets is allocated to common equities, while 68% of RLTY's assets are allocated to common equities. So RQI is most aligned to directly participate in the growth of its positions, while RNP and RLTY put a greater emphasis on income generation from its fixed income or preferred securities.

Unfortunately, a direct performance comparison between these peers is a bit flawed due to RLTY's limited operating history. RLTY has an inception dating back to 2022, so its performance window is fairly limited. Looking at the performance below, we can see that RNP has the highest total return, but once again, this doesn't paint a clear picture of the performance over the next ten years. Since RQI has the largest direct allocation to equities, I believe that RQI is the most suited to see the best total returns when interest rates are lower and conditions within the real estate market are healthier.

Risks

When it comes to the risk profile of the fund, RQI continues to trade near the higher end of its historical price to NAV range. When I last covered RQI, the fund traded at a discount to NAV of 3.11%. Due to the share price declining over the last twelve months, RQI now trades at a slightly larger discount to NAV of 3.84%. For reference, RQI has traded at an average discount to NAV of 4.22% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that RQI continues to trade at one of the least attractive price to NAV valuations over its ten-year historical range.

Therefore, the current price to NAV valuation can be an indication that it may not be the best time to accumulate. Looking back over the fund's price to NAV history, there were many better time frames to initiate a position in RQI. For patient investors, there's always a chance that RQI's share price continues to decline since interest rates remain elevated. However, the price to NAV discount is typically a direct reflection of the overall sentiment of the fund. Since RQI has demonstrated the characteristics of a healthy long-term holding, it can be challenging to get the best possible price to NAV entry.

While the assumption is that interest rates will eventually decline over time, we don't necessarily know what the Fed has in store. In a scenario where interest rates do not decline in the near-term, it is certainly possible for the higher cost of debt environment to weigh on RQI's growth potential. While interest rates remain elevated, this will continue to put pressure on RQI's operating spread and can also impact growth initiates for RQI's specific holdings. A higher cost of debt can cause REITs to delay growth initiatives, such as value-add renovations, acquisitions, or even development of new properties.

Income Utilization

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.13 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 9.2%. Looking at the dividend history below, we can see that RQI has a stable history of keeping payouts within a consistent range over the last few years. This is excellent for investors that want to secure a reliable source of monthly dividends, but there's always a chance that payouts can be reduced during periods of challenging market conditions. If REITs continue to struggle over the next twelve months, RQI's earnings may not be able to keep up with the high rate of payouts.

Unfortunately, the fund has not yet released an updated annual report that highlights its financial details. However, we can refer to the latest semi-annual report to get clarity into the fund's earnings. If we take the monthly distribution of $0.13 per share and multiply it by twelve months, we get an annual payout amount of $1.56 per share. Referring to the latest semi-annual report, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: ($0.40) per share.
  • 2021: $6.06 per share.
  • 2022 ($4.81) per share.
  • 2023: $1.75 per share.
  • 2024: $0.74 per share.
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $0.55 per share.

As we can see, the earnings can wildly vary over time, and the fund has actually struggled to generate an efficient level of earnings in recent years. Looking at the earnings history, we can see how the fund has a clear vulnerability to higher interest rates. Back when rates were cut to near zero levels, RQI was able to generate massive annual earnings of $6.06 per share back in 2021. Therefore, RQI has demonstrated its ability to generate several years' worth of payouts in a single instance when market conditions allow. So the sustainability of RQI's distributions relies on a healthier interest rate environment.

2021's earnings of $6.06 per share/$1.56 annual payout = 3.88 years.

As an added bonus, RQI has a history of paying out a portion of its distributions in a tax-efficient manner. For instance, the latest section 19(a) notice indicates that 54.1% of the distributions paid YTD were classified as long-term capital gains. Long-term capital gains are classified as qualified dividends and have the most favorable tax treatment for long-term investors. While the remaining portion of distributions were classified as net investment income and short-term capital gains, the inclusion of long-term capital gains can help soften the overall debt burden for income investors.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on RQI at this time. Despite the fund's underwhelming price to NAV valuation, RQI continues to be an excellent choice to navigate the real estate sector. For instance, RQI has outperformed XLRE over the last ten years due to its ability to keep payouts consistent. Since RQI has direct equity exposure to some of the highest quality REITs out there, the fund is aligned to see growth as the real estate sector improves. A lower interest rate environment will impact the fund's operating spread and can serve as a growth catalyst for many of the fund's top holdings. Additionally, RQI provides exposure to data center REITs, which may see rapid growth over the next few years as the demand for AI increases. However, the fund's earnings have remained variable over the last few years and the fund has struggled to generate earnings that can outpace distributions over the last two years. Therefore, RQI's performance can remain poor if interest rates do not decline over the next twelve months.

data.title: NovaBay: 'Hold' Rating As New M&A Direction Could Evolve But With Risks
data.description:
I'm initiating coverage on NovaBay Pharmaceuticals (NBY) with a “Hold” rating. The reason why is because the company made a series of moves in 2025, selling off major assets from its pipeline. In the most recent quarterly report, it noted that it had sold off its assets of DERMAdoctor, Avenova, and PhaseOne. The initial plan was to dissolve the whole company and move on. If it sold off its assets, then why is there value left in this company? There is some value due to a change in strategic direction that has happened. The prior Chief Executive Officer (CEO), David Lazar, was able to announce a $6 million financing deal to keep the company going in August of 2025. Even though this was the CEO announced at that time, later in October of 2025 there was a leadership change. Michael Kazley was appointed as the new CEO and Chairman after several board resignations. Another positive is that the company was able to keep its stock listed on the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE). Thus, part of the value is that there is speculation that the company could acquire a new asset or end up with some type of strategic merger.

The stock gained 103% this past Friday as high volume and speculation ramped up. The premise is also that the float is low. Despite there being a total of roughly 126 million shares outstanding in stock, the float stands at just 12.39 million. The truth is that the vote to dissolve the company was there, but the CEO had a different idea in mind. There is “risk” here, and that's why I can't recommend it as a buy. A “Hold” rating is more in order because if something does materialize, then shareholders would be rewarded. On the flip side, there is never a guarantee that a deal or merger will take place. Thus, in the end it is very possible that this company could just dissolve itself in the end anyways. With the selling of its core skin care and other assets like DERMAdoctor, Avenova, and PhaseOne, plus the potential to acquire a product/merge with another company, I believe that NovaBay Pharmaceuticals should be initiated with a “Hold” rating.

Several Moves Line Up Possible Strategic Change For New Chapter

As I noted above, NovaBay Pharmaceuticals throughout 2025 had divested a lot of its assets. This means it got rid of its original business and is now evaluating several strategic alternatives. What I'm going to go over in this section are several items that likely collectively caused the stock price to trade higher by 103% this past Friday to around $19.16 per share. With the biggest one being the momentum of a possible transaction to bring about a major change to the company.

The first reason or move that may have led to the eventual rise in stock price would have been the divestiture of the company's assets. The company moved quickly in 2025 to sell off its products as part of its pipeline. The three products in question are as follows:

  • DERMAdoctor
  • Avenova
  • PhaseOne

The sale of Avenova as part of its eye unit was closed on January 17, 2025. This product contained pure hypochlorous acid (HOCl) and was responsible for treating patients' eyes. Specifically, it was used as an eyelid and eyelash cleaner for certain disorders such as the following:

  • Dry eye disease (DED)
  • Blepharitis
  • Other lid disorders where bacteria/debris played a role

With the deal itself being closed in the early part of 2025, this was the third and final deal that had gone through. The second deal happened around the same time frame, where on January 8, 2025, it sold off its PhaseOne unit. As far as the first divestiture of assets, this dealt with the selling off of its primary skin business, DERMAdoctor, on March 12, 2024. The point here is that this is the first item to show that this company went in a different strategic direction.

The second move that may have led to the rise in stock price would be the board of the company itself implementing a new direction. Why do I believe this to be the case? Quite simply, the vote to move forward with dissolution was there. If the Board deemed it appropriate to do so, it could have just dissolved the remaining company. However, it was decided against making such a move by the Board, and thus, setting up what ultimately led to the recent surge in stock price. The Plan of Dissolution vote happened at the 2025 special meeting on April 16, 2025, and was approved after. That's why the Board could have just agreed to move forward dissolving the company as opposed to continuing on as a business entity.

The third reason is because of the prior CEO being implemented for NovaBay Pharmaceuticals. In addition to the cash flow that came along with him at that period. What happened is that David Lazar became the new CEO of this company in August of 2025. Besides being a new leader for the company, he had made a $6 million investment. However, as I noted above, there was a new CEO appointed shortly thereafter in October of 2025 by the name of Michael Kazley. The prior CEO, though, drove much of the speculative merger and/or acquisition that the company was to focus on. This was the stated move for investment and the new role in the press release by the prior CEO, David Lazar, as follows:

“In making this significant investment in NovaBay, I look forward to maintaining NovaBay’s public listing and actively exploring strategic opportunities to drive value for our stockholders”

The hope is that it can somehow enact a strategic alternative that might be beneficial to NovaBay shareholders. For example, it could end up being an acquisition to bring forth a new product/pipeline, a reverse merger, or the selling of the company itself. On the flip side, what is important to note is that there is no guarantee of such a strategic alternative happening. Especially if the new CEO, Michael Kazley, and the company end up taking a different direction. Although the stock price has traded higher on speculation that something could possibly be announced soon.

The fourth reason for the high interest in the company's stock would be on the basis of a positive listing outcome by the NYSE and also additional funding. The original plan was dissolution for sure, as I have laid out above. However, if the company were to dissolve, I don't believe it would look to stay afloat on the NYSE for an extended period of time. Well, that's exactly what transpired relating to its listing on this exchange. The problem stemmed from April 18, 2024, when it was notified by this exchange that it was not in compliance with the minimum stockholders' equity. However, things have gone well since then, as the company was able to regain its footing with respect to this issue. With that being said, on October 20, 2025, it was notified by the NYSE that it did meet the requirements for continued listing. This is important because it is just one of many items I have gone over above that puts the possibility of dissolution out of the realm for now. That's not to say that things can't go south later on, because they still can.

Financials

Another point to make with NovaBay Pharmaceuticals and why it is in a different position is because of its financials. With the $393,000 it had in cash, plus the net proceeds from the issuance of Series E Preferred Stock and October 2025 Pre-Funded Warrants, it believes that it has enough cash on hand to fund its operations through November 7, 2026. This is less than a 1-year's worth of cash runway, and as such, I believe that it is going to dilute to raise cash very soon. The first reason is because its stock price traded higher by 103% to $19.16 per share on increased volume and speculation. Thus, management is going to take advantage of this and likely raise funds. Plus, there is already a financial tool in place that NovaBay could choose to utilize if it deems it necessary to do so.

This would be with respect to the S-3/A registration of securities that took place on November 25, 2025, and then was declared effective by the SEC the very next day on November 26, 2025. The proposal for this is that NovaBay could sell up to $200 million worth of units (mixed as stock, preferred stock, debt securities, warrants, and units) in order to raise funds. It doesn't have to use this, but my guess is that it might, considering the run-up it had in its share price over the last few weeks (especially with Friday's gain of 103%). The thing is that this company, even with some product revenue, is operating at a loss each quarter. In the most recent quarter, it showed that for the 3 months ending September 30, 2025, it lost $1.25 million (operating loss 10-Q SEC Filing, page #35).

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in NovaBay Pharmaceuticals. The first risk to consider would be the huge jump in share price of the company. High volume came in, and it was on the basis of speculation that it might be nearing a merger and/or acquisition transaction. The risk here is that there is no guarantee that such a transaction is going to take place. Even if a merger or strategic alternative is achieved, there is no assurance that it will be on acceptable terms and that shareholders will end up being happy with it.

The second risk goes to the notion of its cash on hand. As I stated above, it received a $6 million investment from the prior CEO, David Lazar, so that it didn't have to dissolve. Along with the other financial transactions that the company had done afterwards to raise cash, like issuing Series E Preferred Stock and the October 2025 Pre-Funded Warrants that it had issued. It believes that it has enough cash on hand to fund its operations through November 7, 2026. The risk here is that there is no assurance that it will be able to continue to receive additional cash from other sources. Especially since it really doesn't have a pipeline anymore. In addition, there is risk of dilution for shareholders as well. That is, management might choose to raise funds in order to continue to fund itself as a business entity.

Both of the risks above are ones worth considering, but there is a third and final risk here that might be a possibility. That is, the risk of bankruptcy. The intention is to forgo the dissolution and enact some type of strategic alternative. There is no guarantee that it will be able to find a company wanting to merge or perform a reverse merger for starters. Nor that it will be able to acquire a new product or pipeline to keep itself going as a fully-fledged biotech company. If management fails to secure some type of transaction, then there is a chance the company could just end up being dissolved. If this were to happen, then shareholders would get back much less than they invested.

Conclusion

NovaBay Pharmaceuticals was able to divest several of its assets, as I have stated above. These include DERMAdoctor, Avenova, and PhaseOne. The thing is that this company is just trading around speculation at the moment. Plus, the high volume was seen in this stock this past Friday (January 9, 2025). Its average trading volume is only 1.2 million shares, but several days ago, in one day, it reached 10 million shares. Today, though (January 12, 2025), the stock is trading lower by 33% on 2.37 million volume. This brings up the risk noted above in that the stock climbed on speculation of a merger/acquisition. Thus, at the same rate the stock traded higher on the back of this, it could immediately reverse lower. With a change in direction starting off with the prior CEO David Lazard (plus a $6 million investment from him), along with the ability to possibly implement a strategic alternative that might happen under new CEO Michael Kazley, I believe that NovaBay's stock should be initiated with a “Hold” rating. I can't condone a “Buy” or “Strong Buy” rating because at the present time there is nothing super concrete about its long-term prospects.

data.title: U.S. December CPI: Core Goods Weakness Continued To Undermine Tariff Concerns
data.description:
The December CPI report closes out the year with a soft view of inflationary pressures. The headline figure was shaped by the volatile components, where a sharp move in food prices was largely offset by a more muted energy print, keeping overall inflation from shifting much. But the more important signal this month came from the core details, where the downside surprise was driven by softer goods inflation even as services, particularly shelter, remained firm. Taken together, the report reinforces that inflation pressures continue to cool in aggregate, while also offering an important insight on a key policy question: whether tariffs are translating into sustained consumer price acceleration, or only creating temporary noise in a handful of import-sensitive categories.

CPI

U.S. CPI increased 0.3% MoM and 2.7% YoY in December, meeting analyst expectations that the headline inflation rate would be basically unchanged. The annual rate of 2.7% is unchanged from the contentious November print and consistent with readings in June and July of last year. There were competing forces within the volatile components that offset each other, keeping the headline inflation rate from moving much.

  • The food component increased 0.7% MoM in December, the largest monthly increase since October 2022, driven by the food at home index increasing at the same high rate of 0.7% MoM, the highest monthly increase since August 2022. There was breadth in the gains in grocery prices, with 5/6 of the major indexes rising. The smallest subsegment, other food at home, saw the largest increase of 1.6% MoM as sugar & sweets (+0.8% MoM), fats & oils (+1.8% MoM), and spices, condiments & sauces (+1.9% MoM) printed hot. The smaller food-away-from-home index was also up 0.7% MoM, the largest since October 2022.
  • Offsetting the strong gains in food prices was a cooler energy segment increase of 0.3% MoM in December. Within the index, a large 4.4% MoM increase in the natural gas index was offset by gas prices down -0.5% MoM (NSA gas price index down -5.3% MoM) and the electricity index down -0.1% MoM. The monthly rise in utility natural gas services was the largest since January 2023.

The standout among the volatile components was a surprisingly strong monthly increase in the food segment. This likely reflects an underestimation of the November Food index rather than a meaningful shift in underlying inflation dynamics. In last month’s partial report, the BLS showed food prices rising just 0.06% from September to November, which effectively implied near-zero monthly gains in both October and November. Against that backdrop, the hot December increase looks more like a statistical payback from an unusually soft November reading than evidence of a broader reacceleration in food inflation.

Core CPI

U.S. core CPI inflation, which excludes food and energy, increased 0.2% MoM and 2.6% YoY in December, missing expectations of a 0.3% MoM monthly print and the annual rate accelerating to 2.7% YoY. The downside surprise was mostly a result of goods segments coming in weaker than expected, while services segments posted another month of moderate gains. This continues a string of prints that point to cooling core price pressures and an annual rate that is the lowest since March 2021.

Here are some relevant details within core CPI:

  • The core goods segment saw no change on a monthly basis in December, the weakest since May 2025. However, there was variability in the monthly rates of the indexes within the core goods segment. Household goods (+0.5% MoM) and apparel (+0.6% MoM) both posted strong increases in December, and medical care goods (+0.3% MoM) saw a moderate rise. These were offset by declines in transportation goods (-0.3% MoM), education & communication goods (-2.0% MoM), and alcoholic beverages (-0.1% MoM).
  • Two key indexes that drove the softness in core goods were the new vehicles and the used cars & trucks indexes, printing 0.0% MoM and -1.1% MoM, respectively. The decline in the latter index was the largest since July 2024. Weakness in vehicle prices has been a factor in weakening core inflation, likely driven by consumers’ demand for autos softening in the face of a weakening labor market.
  • The core services segment increased 0.3% MoM in December, the hottest since August. The shelter index was the main reason for this, rising 0.4% MoM (also the hottest since August), but not necessarily all due to rent and home prices. Lodging away from home jumped 2.9% MoM, the strongest since September 2023. Outside of shelter, there were moderate to strong increases in medical care services (+0.4% MoM), transportation services (+0.5% MoM), and recreation services (+1.8% MoM; largest ever)

Going through the details of the core components, it’s clear that there was a lot of variability in both services and goods prices in December. The larger core subsegments, new & used motor vehicles (-0.4% MoM) and shelter (+0.4% MoM) came close to offsetting each other, which suggests that other indexes were in aggregate pointing to a slight weakening in core price pressures. Indeed, the BLS prints a CPI excluding food, energy, used vehicles, and shelter index, and it was up just 0.2% MoM and 2.3% YoY, just slightly above the Fed’s 2% target.

In the month’s search for evidence that tariffs are causing a significant uptick, we find very little. Yes, household goods and apparel both did see moderately strong increases in December (both indexes that should be sensitive to import duties on consumer goods), but the broader short-term trend in core goods inflation is turning over. Specifically, the 3-month annualized rate is down to 1.1%, the lowest since June. This looks more like evidence that tariffs caused a temporary increase in inflation, consistent with the Fed’s base case.

Market Reaction

Markets have been slightly volatile in trading the CPI data, but the general bias has been dovish. That bias has been driven by the slight downside surprise of core CPI inflation, overshadowing the hot prints in food and shelter.

  • S&P futures (SPX) jumped right after the CPI data was released, but the initial rise was sold off to a more moderate gain. In the end, the S&P 500 (SP500) opened roughly unchanged and has moved lower by about -2% in the first half hour of trading. NASDAQ (COMP:IND) is faring slightly better at around 0.0%, and the small-cap index, the Russell 2000 (RTY), is very slightly higher by 0.1%. The move in equities doesn’t seem to have been impacted by CPI as much as the moves in bond yields.
  • Treasury yields dipped immediately after the release of the CPI, but the move lower was reversed going into the open of the stock market. Yields maintained a slight decline around 10 am; however, with long-end yields like the 10-year seeing larger declines. The differences in the moves along the yield curve likely reflect the bond market attributing slightly weaker inflationary pressure to weaker economic fundamentals.
  • The U.S. dollar has had a similar reaction to short-term bond yields, falling sharply at first before recovering later in the morning. The U.S. dollar index (DXY) is actually slightly positive as of 10 am as it strengthens against several major currencies.

While the CPI report can mostly be assessed as weak, the implications for the Fed are not that important. The benign print still has a bit of uncertainty built into it via the messy November report, and the decision to pause in January (January 28-29th meeting) has looked more or less locked in regardless of this data (FedWatch January pause probability > 95%).
